patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] mm: zswap: free cold writeback folios promptly

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/2] mm: zswap: free cold writeback folios promptly

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] mm: zswap: free synchronous-IO writeback folios directly
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Kairui Song suggested renaming swap_cache_alloc_folio to __swap_cache_alloc_folio and updating its kdoc to inform callers that the folio will not be reclaimed if it is allocated off-LRU, and also asked whether adding a bool argument to skip_lru would improve the code.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: The reviewer suggested adding a check to ensure the folio is not dirty or swapped out before freeing it, citing potential issues with racing swapin operations and incorrect behavior in certain edge cases.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm I think you also have to test for writeback and dirty here right?

After a racing swapin freed the swap slot, the folio might be dirtied
and swapped out again. We probably shouldn't try to remove_mapping()
such a folio?

We also do similar check for zswap/sync-io devices in pageout handling
(mm/vmscan.c, shrink_folio_list()), and the other dropbehind handler
(filemap_end_dropbehind()).

When I was playing with this, I also tested the folio with
folio_test_active() and folio_mapped(), but I must admit - I do not
remember why I did it. Could just be me making sure we dont get rid of
frequently used folio rather than for strict correctness. :)

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] mm: zswap: deferred dropbehind free of writeback folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of writeback folios in the zswap compression algorithm by deferring their free operation to a workqueue that runs after writeback completion, ensuring they are promptly freed even for asynchronous block devices and filesystem-backed swap. This prevents cold folios from being left on the LRU for reclaim to clean up later. The patch modifies the `folio_end_writeback` function to check if the folio is off-LRU due to zswap writeback and, if so, defers its free operation using a new function `zswap_writeback_dropbehind_folio`. This approach ensures that zswap writeback folios are handled consistently across different I/O types.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch introduces a potential deadlock between the per-vswap spinlock and the folio lock during reclaim paths, and suggested adding a call to zswap_writeback_dropbehind_folio() before try_to_unmap().

Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested batching the free step of zswap writeback folios after remove_mapping() succeeds for each folio, proposing to add the folios to a batch and then call folios_put_refs() in the entire batch.
review_comments.raw_body:
No Suggested-by for poor Nhat? :(

Just kidding, of course - thanks for working on this cool idea :)

---

nit: I wonder if it's worth doing some batching here for the free step.

i.e, after remove_mapping() succeeds for a folio, we can just add the
folio to a batch, then call folios_put_refs() in the entire batch :-?

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of zswap by freeing cold writeback folios promptly after writeback completion, reducing unnecessary reclaim scans and keeping memory resident for shorter periods. The patch consists of two parts: one for synchronous-IO devices (zram, brd, pmem) that can free the folio directly in the calling context, and another for asynchronous block devices and filesystem-backed swap that allocate the folio off the LRU and defer the free to a workqueue. The results show significant improvements in performance metrics such as page rotations, steals, scans, and build times, with most folios being freed promptly on writeback completion.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Kairui Song questioned whether the patch's approach of adding a new argument to swap_cache_alloc_folio and then calling folio_add_lru() would be more effective than simply using the existing bool skip_lru, without testing it.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham noted that the patch may incorrectly free a folio if it has been dirtied and swapped out again, suggesting additional checks be added to ensure the folio is not being reused before freeing it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm I think you also have to test for writeback and dirty here right?

After a racing swapin freed the swap slot, the folio might be dirtied
and swapped out again. We probably shouldn't try to remove_mapping()
such a folio?

We also do similar check for zswap/sync-io devices in pageout handling
(mm/vmscan.c, shrink_folio_list()), and the other dropbehind handler
(filemap_end_dropbehind()).

When I was playing with this, I also tested the folio with
folio_test_active() and folio_mapped(), but I must admit - I do not
remember why I did it. Could just be me making sure we dont get rid of
frequently used folio rather than for strict correctness. :)

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Nhat Pham made a lighthearted comment about not being included in the Suggested-by list, but did not raise any technical concerns or suggestions.

Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested batching the free step after remove_mapping() succeeds for each folio, proposing to add the folio to a batch and then call folios_put_refs() in the entire batch.
review_comments.raw_body:
No Suggested-by for poor Nhat? :(

Just kidding, of course - thanks for working on this cool idea :)

---

nit: I wonder if it's worth doing some batching here for the free step.

i.e, after remove_mapping() succeeds for a folio, we can just add the
folio to a batch, then call folios_put_refs() in the entire batch :-?

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the implementation is too specific to zswap and suggested adding general support for swap-backed pages in folio_end_dropbehind() before having zswap opt into it, also pointing out a challenge of handling interrupt context.
review_comments.raw_body:
I took a quick look through the patches and the implementation seems
too zswap-specific.

I am not super familiar with how the drop-behind mechanism works in
general, but it seems like it's currently intended for file pages and
not swap-backed pages. From a high-level perspective, it seems like we
should add support for swap dropbehind in general first (regardless of
zswap), e.g. by making folio_end_dropbehind() properly handle them,
and then have zswap opt into this?

I guess one of the challenges is that folio_end_dropbehind() only
frees folios in task context while we need to support interrupt
context, but this also doesn't seem like something that should be
handled in zswap.

In theory, there may be a use case for doing dropbehind for swap in
general, right?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 00/36] Private Memory NUMA Nodes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/36] mm: refactor find_next_best_node to find_next_best_node_in
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the find_next_best_node function to make it more flexible by allowing it to take an explicit list of candidates instead of just the N_MEMORY set. This change enables building fallback lists that include non-N_MEMORY nodes, but does not introduce any functional changes as all current callers still select from N_MEMORY. The refactor involves renaming the function to find_next_best_node_in and updating several calls in memory-tiers.c and page_alloc.c to use the new function.

review_comments.author: Balbir Singh
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/36] mm/page_alloc: refactor build_node_zonelist() out of build_zonelists()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the build_node_zonelist() function out of build_zonelists(). The new function builds a per-node fallback list from a candidate nodemask, allowing zonelists to be built from these lists instead of just the default N_MEMORY node state list. This change does not introduce any functional changes and maintains the same behavior as before. The patch also updates various functions to use the new build_node_zonelist() function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/36] mm/page_alloc: let the bulk and folio allocators carry alloc_flags
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the bulk and folio allocators to carry alloc_flags, which enables callers to select allocator behavior such as an alternate zonelist. The patch adds new functions in page_alloc.h that take an explicit alloc_flags parameter, matching the existing __alloc_pages_noprof function. The public gfp.h wrappers remain flag-free. This change does not introduce any functional changes, as all callers currently pass ALLOC_DEFAULT.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/36] numa: introduce N_MEMORY_PRIVATE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new node type called N_MEMORY_PRIVATE to support devices that want to hotplug memory onto a node without exposing it for general consumption. The patch adds infrastructure for private nodes, including a per-node container, functions to check if a page resides on a private node or is managed by a zone device, and registration API for private nodes. It also updates sysfs attributes and helpers to handle private nodes correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/36] mm: add ZONELIST_PRIVATE(_NOFALLBACK) for N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new zonelist type, ZONELIST_PRIVATE(_NOFALLBACK), to isolate N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes from unintentional allocations. It introduces a new alloc_flags flag, ALLOC_ZONELIST_PRIVATE, and modifies the page allocator to use this new zonelist when allocating pages from private nodes. The patch also adds explicit allocation functions for private nodes to prevent open-coding. This change makes it impossible for normal callers of page_alloc to reach N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes unless explicitly called for.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/36] cpuset: exclude private nodes from cpuset.mems (default-open)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's cpuset functionality to exclude private nodes from being managed by cpusets. Private nodes are treated as globally accessible resources, rather than being partitioned among cpusets. The patch also ensures that memory policies are re-bound when a private node leaves N_MEMORY_PRIVATE state on hot-unplug. This change aims to prevent issues with silent background operations and migrations due to cpuset membership changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/36] mm/memory_hotplug: disallow migration-driven private node hotunplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the hotunplug of private node memory that is being migrated, as this can lead to unsafe conditions. Instead of attempting to hotunplug the memory, it fails the operation and requires the owner to safely drain the folios before proceeding. This change breaks out of the hotunplug attempt entirely when a fatal migration failure occurs, rather than spinning indefinitely on a failed migration attempt.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/36] mm/mempolicy: skip private node folios when queueing for migration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory policy module to skip private node folios when queueing them for migration. Private nodes are not included in policy nodemasks, but could still be encountered during an mbind(MPOL_MF_MOVE) walk. The patch adds a filter to prevent mempolicy-driven migration from moving private-node memory, similar to the existing filter for ZONE_DEVICE folios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/36] mm/migrate: disallow userland driven migration for private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents userland-driven migration of memory on private nodes by adding a check to the migration code. The change uses the same filter locations as zone device folios, but instead checks for private managed memory. This ensures that sensitive data on private nodes is not accidentally migrated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/36] mm/madvise: disallow madvise operations on private node folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents madvise operations from being performed on private node memory by adding filters to the huge pages and zone device locations. It does this by checking if a folio is part of a private node before allowing madvise operations, effectively disallowing these operations on such memory. This change aims to prevent potential issues that could arise from madvise operations on private node memory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/36] mm/compaction: disallow compaction on private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents compaction from occurring on private nodes in the Linux kernel's memory management system. Compaction requires the ability to migrate pages, which is not yet supported on private nodes. The patch adds checks to skip compaction on private nodes in several functions related to compaction and node management.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/36] mm/page_alloc: clear private node watermarks and system reserves
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation code to exclude private nodes from system reserve calculations and set their watermarks to zero. Private nodes are not supported for reclaim, making their watermarks pointless. Including them in system reserve calculations can dilute DRAM watermarks and lead to poor OOM kill behavior. The changes aim to improve the OOM killer's ability to resolve general system pressure by releasing private node capacity.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/36] mm/mempolicy: disallow NUMA Balancing prot_none on private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory policy to prevent NUMA balancing on private nodes, treating them similarly to zone device memory. This is because private nodes do not currently support migration. The change affects how folios are mapped for protection.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/36] mm/damon: skip private node memory in DAMON migration and pageout
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the DAMON (Data Access Monitor) kernel module to skip private node memory during migration and pageout operations. Private node memory is not reclaimable by default, so this change ensures that DAMON does not attempt to migrate or reclaim such memory. The patch adds checks for private node memory in both the migration and pageout paths of DAMON's code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/36] mm/ksm: skip KSM for managed-memory folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the kernel's KSM (Kernel Samepage Merging) functionality to skip merging and scanning operations for private memory folios, which are intended for explicit placement. This change prevents KSM from operating on private node folios by default, thus maintaining the purpose of private memory. The modification uses a filter location similar to that used for zone_device folios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/36] mm/khugepaged: skip private node folios when trying to collapse.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the khugepaged functionality to skip private node folios when attempting to collapse huge pages. The collapse operation can cause new THP allocations and memory migrations, which are handled similarly to zone_device pages by disallowing collapse. This change aims to prevent potential issues arising from these operations on private node folios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/36] mm/vmscan: disallow reclaim of private node memory
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the Linux kernel from attempting to reclaim memory on private nodes by adding a filter in the shrink_node function. Private nodes do not support reclaim by default, so this change ensures that reclaimers such as direct and kswapd do not operate on them. The approach taken is to check if a node is private at the common chokepoint for most reclaimers, preventing unnecessary work and potential issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/36] mm/gup: disallow longterm pin of private node folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents long-term pinning of folios on private nodes, which would lead to incorrect behavior in the GUP long-term path. It introduces a new function `folio_longterm_pin_forbidden()` that returns true for any private node folio and uses this function to reject such pins before migration. The gup-fast path is also updated to defer private folios to the slow path via `folio_allows_longterm_pin()`. This change ensures that private node folios are neither pinned nor migrated, preventing potential issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 19/36] proc: include N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes in numa_maps output
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the numa_maps output in Linux to include private nodes that are not part of the N_MEMORY state. This is achieved by adding a check for N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes in the page-table walkers and modifying the show_numa_map function to print these nodes. The goal is to make private-node mappings visible in numa_maps, which was previously not possible due to filtering based on N_MEMORY.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 20/36] mm/memcontrol: account private-node memory in per-node stats
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory control system to account private-node memory in per-node statistics. Currently, private nodes are not included in per-node views of memory usage because their lruvec counters are not refreshed. This patch fixes this issue by including N_MEMORY_PRIVATE in the flush and display process, ensuring that private-node memory is correctly reported per node. The changes affect two files: mm/memcontrol-v1.c and mm/memcontrol.c. The patch is unconditional and does not depend on future capabilities.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 21/36] proc/kcore: include private-node RAM in the kcore RAM map
series_items.patch_summary: The patch modifies the kcore_ram_list function in fs/proc/kcore.c to include private-node RAM in the kcore RAM map, ensuring that all memory is accessible through /proc/kcore.

review_comments.author: Omar Sandoval
review_comments.summary: Acked the patch, suggesting it can be merged through a tree that takes the rest of the series.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jul 20, 2026 at 03:34:15PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> The kcore_ram_list bounds its scan at the highest pfn in N_MEMORY.
> 
> As a result, private nodes placed above the last N_MEMORY node, which is
> the common case for device/CXL memory - is truncated out of /proc/kcore.
> 
> Include N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes in the scan boundary.  /proc/kcore is
> root-only (CAP_SYS_RAWIO), so this exposes nothing new - it just lets
> a debugger read private-node memory like any other RAM.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
> ---
>  fs/proc/kcore.c | 5 ++++-
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/proc/kcore.c b/fs/proc/kcore.c
> index 390547b992acd..f67194dc2a9b8 100644
> --- a/fs/proc/kcore.c
> +++ b/fs/proc/kcore.c
> @@ -251,11 +251,14 @@ static int kcore_ram_list(struct list_head *list)
>  {
>  	int nid, ret;
>  	unsigned long end_pfn;
> +	nodemask_t mem;
>  
>  	/* Not initialized....update now */
>  	/* find out "max pfn" */
>  	end_pfn = 0;
> -	for_each_node_state(nid, N_MEMORY) {
> +	/* Include private node memory in the scan */
> +	nodes_or(mem, node_states[N_MEMORY], node_states[N_MEMORY_PRIVATE]);
> +	for_each_node_mask(nid, mem) {
>  		unsigned long node_end;
>  		node_end = node_end_pfn(nid);
>  		if (end_pfn < node_end)
> -- 
> 2.53.0-Meta
> 

I'm sure this is the least of your concern with this series, but FWIW:

Acked-by: Omar Sandoval <osandov@osandov.com>

If everything goes well, kcore.c is quiet enough that this can probably
just be merged through whatever tree takes the rest of this work.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 22/36] mm/mempolicy: add MPOL_F_PRIVATE and zonelist selection
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, MPOL_F_PRIVATE, to the Linux memory policy system. This flag indicates that a policy contains a nodemask with private nodes and makes these nodes reachable via standard memory policies. The patch also introduces zonelist selection into the mpol allocator interfaces using the alloc_flags carried by page_alloc. With this change, a VMA can successfully service faults from private node memory, and cpuset rebinding does not affect private nodes. The patch is currently a no-op as nothing sets MPOL_F_PRIVATE yet, but it lays the groundwork for future use.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 23/36] mm/mempolicy: apply policy at the kernel zone for private-node binds
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a livelock issue in the Linux kernel's memory policy system by introducing a new function, `policy_private_has_kernel_zone`, which checks if any private node in a policy can satisfy non-movable allocations. The function is used to determine whether to apply the policy at the kernel zone for private-node binds. This change ensures that kernel allocations are not forced onto zones that cannot satisfy them, preventing infinite retries and improving system performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 24/36] mm/mempolicy: add in-kernel MPOL_BIND interfaces for drivers/services
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new interfaces to the Linux kernel's memory policy (mempolicy) module: mpol_bind_node() and mpol_private_bind(). These interfaces allow drivers or services to create a mempolicy that is pinned to a specific NUMA node, either publicly (mpol_bind_node()) or privately (mpol_private_bind()). The patch also updates the mpol_set_nodemask function to handle private nodes correctly. This change enables drivers to manage memory policies for VMAs they control, and provides a way for them to bypass certain security checks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 25/36] mm/memory_hotplug: support N_MEMORY_PRIVATE node hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for hotplugging memory onto a private node controlled by a module. It introduces a new function, add_private_memory_driver_managed(), which allows modules to register as owners of a private node and manage its memory. The patch also updates the common __add_memory_driver_managed() function to only allow in-tree modules to hotplug N_MEMORY nodes. Private nodes are opted out of starting kswapd/kcompactd by default. If the add fails, the registration is undone. Offline_pages() clears both N_MEMORY and N_MEMORY_PRIVATE flags, while offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() drops private node registration once the last node's memory is removed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 26/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_RECLAIM for opted-in private node reclaim
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to opt private nodes into the reclaim process, allowing them to participate in memory management and compaction. The `NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_RECLAIM` flag is introduced to control this behavior, which can be set by drivers for specific workloads. This change enables private nodes to use normal watermarks and allows swap-out operations, while excluding cross-node operations like demotion and promotion. The patch also updates various kernel modules to accommodate the new functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 27/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_USER_NUMA for userland numa controls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a mechanism to allow userland NUMA controls on private nodes in the Linux kernel. It introduces a new capability, NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_USER_NUMA, which allows userspace to place or migrate memory on private nodes. The patch also updates various functions and interfaces to enforce this capability, including mbind(), set_mempolicy(), move_pages(), and migrate_pages(). This change enables more fine-grained control over NUMA management in userland applications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 28/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_HOTUNPLUG for opted-in private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new capability called NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_HOTUNPLUG to private nodes, which allows memory hotplug to migrate folios on these nodes for the purpose of hot-unplugging memory. Without this capability, hotunplug fails if any folio on the node is allocated. The patch also introduces a function `node_allows_hotunplug` that checks whether a node supports hot-unplug based on its capabilities.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 29/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_DEMOTION for private-node tiering demotion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new capability, NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_DEMOTION, to allow private nodes to participate in the tiering demotion hierarchy. When set, memory-tiers will add the node to the demotion set and reclaim's demote path may target it. This requires CAP_RECLAIM to be enabled as well, which is enforced at registration. The patch also introduces a new function, node_allows_demotion(), to check if a private node allows demotion.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 30/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_NUMA_BALANCING for private-node NUMA balancing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for private-node NUMA balancing in the Linux kernel. It introduces a new capability, NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_NUMA_BALANCING, which allows private nodes to participate in NUMA balancing. The patch also includes changes to the node_private.h and internal.h files to implement this new capability. Unlike demotion, NUMA balancing is not reclaim-driven, so this capability stands alone and does not require CAP_RECLAIM. This change enables access-based promotion/migration of private nodes, allowing them to participate in NUMA balancing like normal nodes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 31/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_LTPIN for private node folio pinning
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to opt-in private nodes for long-term GUP pinning, allowing them to be pinned like ordinary memory or migrated and pinned if in a movable zone. The change introduces a new flag NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_LTPIN and modifies existing functions to check for this capability. This enables more flexible handling of private node folios and allows for better utilization of system resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 32/36] mm/khugepaged: base private node collapse eligiblity on actor/cap bits
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the khugepaged daemon's behavior to base its decision on collapsing private nodes on the actor/capability bits. Specifically, it ensures that khugepaged does not silently collapse private node folios and allows MADV_COLLAPSE operations on private nodes opted into CAP_USER_NUMA. The changes are implemented through modifications to the folio_allows_collapse() function in mm/internal.h and its usage in mm/khugepaged.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 33/36] Documentation/mm: describe private (N_MEMORY_PRIVATE) memory nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for private memory nodes, which are NUMA nodes whose memory is hotplugged by a driver and deliberately hidden from the kernel's normal memory management. The documentation covers the isolation model, ZONELIST_PRIVATE, driver provisioning API, capability opt-in model, and observability surfaces. Private memory nodes are marked as N_MEMORY_PRIVATE instead of N_MEMORY and are not considered by the page allocator's normal or fallback paths. This allows drivers to provide a block of NUMA-addressable memory that the rest of the kernel will not allocate from on its own.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 34/36] mm/mempolicy: add mpol_set_shared_policy_range()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function mpol_set_shared_policy_range() that installs an already-built policy over an arbitrary page range in the shared policy tree. The function takes as input the shared policy, start and end page offsets, and a fully-built policy. This allows for more flexibility when binding policies to backing inodes from in-kernel code. Additionally, mpol_set_shared_policy() is reimplemented as a thin wrapper that derives its range from a VMA, sharing an underlying path with the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 35/36] KVM: guest_memfd: bind backing memory to a NUMA node at creation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, GUEST_MEMFD_FLAG_BIND_NODE, to the KVM guest memory file descriptor (memfd) API. When this flag is set, the kernel binds the backing memory of the memfd to a specified NUMA node at creation time, ensuring that all allocated pages are on the same node without requiring userspace to use mbind(). The patch also adds support for this flag in the KVM_CAP_GUEST_MEMFD_FLAGS capability and updates the include files accordingly. This change is only enabled when CONFIG_NUMA is set.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces the concept of 'Private Memory Nodes', which are isolated by default and can be opted into specific services such as page allocation and OOM killing. The goal is to flip the current dynamic where NUMA-hosted memory is accessible by default, and instead isolate it and then opt-in to specific services that the device says is safe. This allows for more secure and efficient use of accelerators, special memory devices, network devices, and other types of memory. The patch reduces complexity and introduces a dedicated zonelist for private nodes, making it easier to manage and control memory access.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the find_next_best_node() function, which picks the next-closest node for a fallback list from the full N_MEMORY set. The author refactored it into find_next_best_node_in(), which takes an explicit candidates nodemask, enabling building fallback lists with non-N_MEMORY candidates. No functional change is made, as every caller still selects from N_MEMORY.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of build_zonelists() by extracting per-node fallback-list construction into a new function, build_node_zonelist(), which builds zonelists from candidate nodemasks instead of just the default N_MEMORY node state list. This change has no functional impact and is intended to simplify the code.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the lack of explicit alloc_flags in bulk and folio allocation functions, explaining that they will add __alloc_pages_bulk_noprof() and modify existing functions to take an alloc_flags parameter, matching the behavior of __alloc_pages_noprof(). The changes are non-functional and only affect internal flag-carrying primitives.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about mutual exclusivity between N_MEMORY and N_MEMORY_PRIVATE states by explaining that they are mutually exclusive, adding checks to ensure this in node_private_register(), and clarifying the behavior of node_state() and helpers when CONFIG_NUMA is not set. A fix is planned for v2.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about unintentional allocations reaching N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes by making these nodes invisible to ordinary allocation and adding explicit private-node alloc() functions, but noted that private-node allocations can still fall back to non-private nodes.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that cpuset operations should not partition private nodes via cpuset.mem and instead treat them as globally accessible resources. The author explained that isolating by cpuset offers no functionality and creates issues on rebind when a cpuset loses access to a node, generating migrations that may not be supported. They also stated that silent background operations on these nodes due to cpuset.mem membership changing should be avoided. A fix is planned for the next version.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about migrating private node memory without coordination by changing the hotunplug attempt to fail instead of spinning forever on a failed migration attempt, and requiring the owner to drain the folios safely before allowing hotunplug to proceed.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about private nodes being moved by mempolicy-driven migration, explaining that they are already kept out of policy nodemasks and adding a filter to skip private-node folios during migration. A fix is planned for the next version.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about userland driven migration requests for private node memory, which should be disallowed. The author has modified the mm/migrate.c file to use the same filter locations as zone_device folios to achieve this.

review_comments.author: Omar Sandoval
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Omar Sandoval noted that kcore.c may not require explicit handling in this series, suggesting it could be merged without additional changes to kcore.c.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm sure this is the least of your concern with this series, but FWIW:

Acked-by: Omar Sandoval <osandov@osandov.com>

If everything goes well, kcore.c is quiet enough that this can probably
just be merged through whatever tree takes the rest of this work.

review_comments.author: Balbir Singh
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not provide a clear explanation of how the new capabilities are being used, and requested additional documentation to clarify this.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks,
Balbir

review_comments.author: Balbir Singh
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/3] mm: move internal mempolicy APIs to new internal header
Re: [PATCH 1/3] mm: move internal mempolicy APIs to new internal header
Re: [PATCH v2 01/15] mm/vma: introduce VMA virtual page offset field and add helpers
Re: [PATCH v2 01/15] mm/vma: introduce VMA virtual page offset field and add helpers
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch introduces a VMA virtual page offset field and adds helpers, but the description of its behavior is unclear.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the clarity of the patch description and suggested rephrasing it to avoid complex combinations of negatives.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jul 20, 2026 at 05:56:52PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes (ARM) wrote:
> On Mon, Jul 20, 2026 at 11:49:00AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Mon, Jul 20, 2026 at 03:38:27PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes (ARM) wrote:
> > >
> > > The virtual page offset of a VMA is equal to vma->vm_start >> PAGE_SHIFT if
> > > they are unfaulted or were not remapped, otherwise it is equal to this
> > > value at the point of first fault.
> > >
> >
> > The latter half of this description is a bit confusing.
> >
> > Why would the value differ from (vma->vm_start >> PAGE_SHIFT)?
> > (because it's file backed?
> 
> Because the VMA gets remapped.
> 
> If it's remapped when it's unfaulted, then virtual page offset gets updated, if
> it's faulted then not.
> 

ahhhhh the wording is just wonky then

when faulted or remapped, x is...
when unfaulted or unremapped, x is...

instead of

x is ... if unfaulted or were not remapped, otherwise...

Sort of a complex combination of 3 negatives there.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes (ARM)
review_comments.summary: Clarified that the VMA virtual page offset field can change when the VMA is remapped or faulted, and acknowledged Gregory's concerns about the description.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/3] mm, treewide: replace __folio_alloc_node() with folio_alloc_node()
Re: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/20] iomap: convert to in-iter iomap_next() model

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/20] iomap: convert to in-iter iomap_next() model

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/20] iomap: split iomap_iter() logic into iomap_iter_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the iomap_iter() logic into a new helper function, iomap_iter_next(). The purpose of this change is to prepare for an upcoming modification that will use an in-iter model, where the ->iomap_next() callback can directly call iomap_iter_next(). The patch adds an inlined helper function and provides macros to generate boilerplate code for ->iomap_next() wrappers. No functional changes are intended, but there is a minor difference on the error path of iomap_end(), which now zeroes iter.status instead of leaving it as the caller's last value.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/20] iomap: add ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new callback function called ->iomap_next() in the iomap API. This function replaces the existing ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks, allowing for more efficient iteration over mappings. The ->iomap_next() callback is responsible for finishing the previous mapping if necessary and producing the next mapping. This change enables performance-critical callers to inline their iteration loop and pass their ->iomap_next() function as a compile-time constant, which can be devirtualized by the compiler into a direct call instead of an indirect call through a function pointer. The patch also updates several files to use the new callback function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/20] iomap: skip the finishing iomap_iter() on the simple DIO read fast path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the direct I/O read path by skipping an unnecessary iomap_iter() call when reading from filesystems that don't require ->iomap_end() work. It introduces a new flag, IOMAP_DIO_NO_IOMAP_END, which allows filesystems to promise there is no ->iomap_end() work and no folio batch, allowing the finishing iomap_iter() to be skipped. The patch applies this optimization to ext4, xfs, exfat, and ntfs direct read paths.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/20] xfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the XFS iomap operations to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using macros that wrap the iomap_iter_next() helper. This change does not introduce any functional changes and is a conversion from the previous ->iomap_begin method. The patch affects several files in the xfs filesystem code, including xfs_file.c and xfs_iomap.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/20] btrfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the Btrfs direct I/O operations to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated by a macro that wraps the iomap_iter_next helper and finishes previous mappings as needed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/20] ntfs3: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the ntfs3 file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END() macro. This change does not introduce any functional changes but rather adapts the existing iomap operations to conform to the new interface. The update affects the inode.c file in the fs/ntfs3 directory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/20] ntfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for NTFS to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using macros that wrap the iomap_iter_next() helper. This change does not introduce any functional changes and only updates the internal implementation of the iomap operations. The conversion is done for several functions: ntfs_read_iomap_ops, ntfs_zero_read_iomap_ops, ntfs_seek_iomap_ops, ntfs_write_iomap_ops, ntfs_page_mkwrite_iomap_ops, and ntfs_dio_iomap_ops.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/20] ext4: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations in the ext4 file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT() macro. This change does not introduce any functional changes and is a conversion to the new API. The macro wraps the iomap_iter_next() helper to finish previous mappings if needed and produce the next one. The patch affects two files: fs/ext4/extents.c and fs/ext4/inode.c, making 9 insertions and 3 deletions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/20] erofs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations in the erofs file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using macros that wrap the iomap_iter_next helper. This change does not introduce any functional changes and is a conversion to the new API. The patch updates two files: data.c and zmap.c, replacing the old callbacks with the new ones.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/20] zonefs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series converts the zonefs iomap operations to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using a macro that wraps the iomap_iter_next() helper.

review_comments.author: Damien Le Moal
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating no issues found.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 11/20] ext2: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for the ext2 file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using a macro that wraps the iomap_iter_next() helper. This change does not introduce any functional changes and is part of a larger series of patches. The conversion is done in the inode.c file of the ext2 file system.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 12/20] block: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for block devices to use a new callback function, ->iomap_next(), which is generated by the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT() macro. This change does not introduce any functional changes and is part of a larger series of patches. The new callback is used to finish previous mappings if necessary and produce the next one.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 13/20] f2fs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for the F2FS file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT() macro. This change does not introduce any functional changes and is a conversion to align with the new API. The patch modifies the data.c file in the fs/f2fs directory by replacing the existing iomap_begin function with the newly defined f2fs_iomap_next function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 14/20] gfs2: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for the GFS2 file system to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using a macro that wraps the iomap_iter_next() helper and finishes previous mappings as needed. This change does not introduce any functional changes, but rather updates the code to conform to the latest kernel interface. The patch modifies the gfs2_iomap_ops structure to use the new ->iomap_next() callback instead of the old ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 15/20] hpfs: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations for the HPFS filesystem to use the new ->iomap_next() callback. This callback is generated using a macro that wraps an existing helper function, and it ensures that any unfinished mappings are completed before producing the next one. The change does not introduce any functional changes, only updating the interface used by the HPFS filesystem.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 16/20] fuse: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the iomap operations in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) code to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is a more efficient way of handling file mappings. The conversion uses macros to generate the necessary callbacks and does not introduce any functional changes. This change affects three files: fs/fuse/dax.c, fs/fuse/file.c, and fs/fuse/virtio_fs.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 17/20] exfat: convert iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the ExFAT filesystem's iomap operations to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which is generated using macros that wrap the iomap_iter_next() helper. This change does not introduce any functional changes and only updates the internal implementation of the iomap operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 18/20] iomap: remove ->iomap_begin()/->iomap_end() legacy path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the legacy ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() path in the iomap code, as all in-tree filesystems have implemented the new ->iomap_next() interface. The changes affect several files, including fs/iomap/direct-io.c and include/linux/iomap.h. The removal of this legacy path simplifies the code and reduces complexity.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 19/20] iomap: pass iomap_iter_next_fn directly instead of struct iomap_ops
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the iomap code by removing the struct iomap_ops, which only contained a single function pointer. Instead of passing an ops struct to functions like iomap_iter(), filesystems can pass their iomap_next() callback directly. This change is possible because all filesystems now implement ->iomap_next() and have removed legacy fallbacks. The patch also renames the 'ops' field in the iomap_iter tracepoint to 'next'.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 20/20] Documentation: iomap: update docs to reflect iomap_iter_next model
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the iomap documentation to reflect a change in how filesystems interact with the iomap API. Specifically, it removes the separate ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks and instead uses a single iomap_iter_next() callback for every operation. This callback finishes the previous mapping (if any) and produces the next one. The patch also updates the documentation to reflect this change and provides information on how filesystems can implement the new callback using the iomap_iter_next helper.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the iomap code to use an iterator model, replacing the current begin/end pair of callbacks with a single next callback. This change allows for better performance by enabling inlining and devirtualization of the iteration loop, as well as providing more flexibility for future callers to express custom logic in the IO path. The patches are broken down into 20 parts, including refactoring existing code, converting each filesystem, removing legacy paths, and updating documentation.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the iomap_iter() function's return semantics, acknowledged that it needs to zero iter.status on error paths, and confirmed no functional change is intended.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the interaction between ->iomap_next() and ->iomap_end(), explaining that iomap_iter() uses ->iomap_next() when available, but falls back to ->iomap_begin()/->iomap_end() if not. The author did not indicate any plans for a fix.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Fengnan Chang pointed out that the ->iomap_next() model causes an extra indirect call and iomap_iter() frames on every I/O for DIO reads on filesystems without a ->iomap_end() work, such as ext4. Author Joanne Koong responded by adding a new flag IOMAP_DIO_NO_IOMAP_END to allow callers to promise there is no ->iomap_end() work and no folio batch, letting iomap_dio_simple() skip the finishing iomap_iter() on both success and error-exit paths.
sentiment_signals: added new code to address issue

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the xfs iomap_ops implementation, specifically that it was not using the new ->iomap_next() callback as intended. The author explained that each callback is generated with macros that wrap the iomap_iter_next() helper to finish the previous mapping if needed and produce the next one.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the conversion of btrfs to the new ->iomap_next() callback, explaining that it is generated using the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END() macro and does not introduce functional changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Convert btrfs iomap_ops to the new ->iomap_next() callback. The callback is
generated with the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END() macro, which wraps the
iomap_iter_next() helper to finish the previous mapping if needed and
produce the next one. No functional changes are intended.

Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
---
 fs/btrfs/direct-io.c | 6 ++++--
 1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/btrfs/direct-io.c b/fs/btrfs/direct-io.c
index 460326d34143..d5439b06cdc9 100644
--- a/fs/btrfs/direct-io.c
+++ b/fs/btrfs/direct-io.c
@@ -798,9 +798,11 @@ static void btrfs_dio_submit_io(const struct iomap_iter *iter, struct bio *bio,
 	btrfs_submit_bbio(bbio, 0);
 }
 
+static DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END(btrfs_dio_iomap_next, btrfs_dio_iomap_begin,
+				  btrfs_dio_iomap_end);
+
 static const struct iomap_ops btrfs_dio_iomap_ops = {
-	.iomap_begin            = btrfs_dio_iomap_begin,
-	.iomap_end              = btrfs_dio_iomap_end,
+	.iomap_next             = btrfs_dio_iomap_next,
 };
 
 static const struct iomap_dio_ops btrfs_dio_ops = {
-- 
2.52.0

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the conversion of ntfs3 to the new ->iomap_next() callback model, explaining that it is generated using the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END() macro and does not introduce any functional changes.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the conversion of the ntfs filesystem's iomap_ops to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, explaining that each callback is generated using the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT()/DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END() macros and no functional changes are intended.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the conversion of ext4 iomap_ops to the new ->iomap_next() callback, explaining that each callback is generated using the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT() macro and no functional changes are intended.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the conversion of erofs iomap_ops to the new ->iomap_next() callback, explaining that each callback is generated using macros and no functional changes are intended.
review_comments.raw_body:
Convert erofs iomap_ops to the new ->iomap_next() callback. Each
callback is generated with the DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT() and
DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END() macros, which wrap the iomap_iter_next()
helper to finish the previous mapping if needed and produce the next
one. No functional changes are intended.

Reviewed-by: Gao Xiang <hsiangkao@linux.alibaba.com>
Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
---
 fs/erofs/data.c | 6 ++++--
 fs/erofs/zmap.c | 5 ++++-
 2 files changed, 8 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/erofs/data.c b/fs/erofs/data.c
index 9aa48c8d67d1..d2f01245ee79 100644
--- a/fs/erofs/data.c
+++ b/fs/erofs/data.c
@@ -380,9 +380,11 @@ static int erofs_iomap_end(struct inode *inode, loff_t pos, loff_t length,
 	return written;
 }
 
+static DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT_END(erofs_iomap_next, erofs_iomap_begin,
+				  erofs_iomap_end);
+
 static const struct iomap_ops erofs_iomap_ops = {
-	.iomap_begin = erofs_iomap_begin,
-	.iomap_end = erofs_iomap_end,
+	.iomap_next = erofs_iomap_next,
 };
 
 int erofs_fiemap(struct inode *inode, struct fiemap_extent_info *fieinfo,
diff --git a/fs/erofs/zmap.c b/fs/erofs/zmap.c
index bab521613552..3966b62a7051 100644
--- a/fs/erofs/zmap.c
+++ b/fs/erofs/zmap.c
@@ -821,6 +821,9 @@ static int z_erofs_iomap_begin_report(struct inode *inode, loff_t offset,
 	return 0;
 }
 
+static DEFINE_IOMAP_ITER_NEXT(z_erofs_iomap_next_report,
+			      z_erofs_iomap_begin_report);
+
 const struct iomap_ops z_erofs_iomap_report_ops = {
-	.iomap_begin = z_erofs_iomap_begin_report,
+	.iomap_next = z_erofs_iomap_next_report,
 };
-- 
2.52.0

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] cgroup/cpu: document cpu.stat nice_usec and core_sched.force_idle_usec
Re: [PATCH] hugetlb: fix subpool release race
patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch aims to fix a subpool release race condition in hugetlb, but reviewer Joshua Hahn questions its practical impact and suggests removing the Fixes tag.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Questioned the practical impact of the patch and suggested removing the Fixes tag. Approved of the subpool_is_free check inside the locked section.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Asked the author to consider Joshua's observations and resend with a more accurate changelog.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 20 Jul 2026 07:31:29 -0700 Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com> wrote:

> Hello Yichong, thank you for the patch!
> 
> > unlock_or_release_subpool() drops spool->lock before checking whether the
> > subpool can be freed.  However, subpool_is_free() reads fields that are
> > updated under spool->lock, including count, used_hpages and rsv_hpages.
> > 
> > Another thread can update those fields before the first thread evaluates
> > subpool_is_free(), allowing both threads to observe the final freeable
> > state and release the subpool.
> 
> I don't think that the race you are mentioning can be hit in practice (i.e.
> there is no user-visible change for this) since two threads cannot actually
> observe the freeable state at the same time.
> 
> subpool_is_free() does an early exit on spool->count so it will never reach
> the inside if block and have two threads try to decrement the
> spool->hstate and free the spool twice. I'm wondering if we need the Fixes
> tag in this case.
> 
> I do think that this is not obvious though, so having the subpool_is_free
> check inside the locked section makes sense to me too.
> 
> Reviewed-by: Joshua Hahn <joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com>

Thanks.  Yichong, can you please consider Joshua's observations and
perhaps resend with a more accurate changelog?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/huge_memory: unlock i_mmap_rwsem before releasing after-split folios

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/huge_memory: unlock i_mmap_rwsem before releasing after-split folios

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug where the i_mmap_rwsem lock is not properly released before freeing after-split folios in the huge_memory module. The issue arises when the split relies on the @folio staying locked and in the page cache, but the unlock loop unlocks @folio before releasing the i_mmap_rwsem lock. This can lead to a concurrent final iput() evicting and RCU-freeing the inode before the i_mmap_unlock_read() function touches the i_mmap_rwsem lock, resulting in a slab-use-after-free error. The patch resolves this by unlocking i_mmap_rwsem right after remap_page(), before the loop that unlocks and frees the after-split folios.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: The reviewer pointed out that the patch may have introduced a pre-existing bug, referencing an external link to Sashiko, which appears to be analyzing the patchset.
sentiment_signals: raised a question about potential existing issue
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko might have found a pre-existing bug in there:

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260716095424.471052-1-kirill@shutemov.name

(why is mapping_set_update() a) a macro and b) undocumented?)

review_comments.author: Miaohe Lin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not address the issue of concurrent final iput() evicting and RCU-freeing the inode before i_mmap_unlock_read() touches i_mmap_rwsem, which can lead to a slab-use-after-free bug.
sentiment_signals: concurrent final iput() evicting and RCU-freeing the inode
review_comments.raw_body:
Reviewed-by: Miaohe Lin <linmiaohe@huawei.com>

Thanks.
.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Matthew Wilcox noted that the commit message is unrelated to the original patch and suggested taking it up with a different person.
review_comments.raw_body:
commit 62e72d2cf702

    So make madvise(..., MADV_COLLAPSE) also call xas_set_lru() to pass the
    list_lru which we may want to insert xa_node into later.  And move
    mapping_set_update to mm/internal.h, and turn into a macro to avoid
    including extra headers in mm/internal.h.

Take it up with Kairui.  And whoever it was added that patch to
linux-mm.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Kairui Song suggested moving the i_mmap_unlock_read() call before the after-split unlock loop and making it a function, but initially wanted to make it an inline helper in pagemap.h or internal.h
review_comments.raw_body:
Right, I initially wanted to make it a inline helper, since this function
were a macro and later become a static helper, so it should be super cheap
in the hot path. That requires to include extra headers in internal.h
and that looked a bit more uglier and every internel.h user will have
extra header dependency. Moving it to pagemap.h also looked confusing
along size other mapping_set_* helper, they are completely different
things, and this help should stay in mm/.

So I just partially restored how it was before b64e74e95aa6, It was
never documented though.

We can make it a function for sure, open to suggestions on this.
